question_text: The Statement of Profit and Loss as per Schedule III starts with:
explanation: As per Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013, the Statement of Profit and Loss begins with 'Revenue from Operations'. This is the first line item, representing the core business income of the company.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Other Income comes after Revenue from Operations as a separate line item.
Total Revenue is the sum of Revenue from Operations and Other Income — it is not the starting point.
Cost of Materials Consumed falls under 'Expenses', which appears after Revenue section.

question_text: Which of the following is NOT included in 'Revenue from Operations' of a manufacturing company?
explanation: Dividend income is not part of Revenue from Operations for a manufacturing company. It is classified as 'Other Income'. Revenue from Operations includes sale of products, sale of services, and other operating revenues directly related to the core business.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Sale of products is the primary revenue stream for a manufacturing company.
Sale of services is part of Revenue from Operations if the company provides services.
Other operating revenues include items like scrap sales, which are part of operations.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing other operating with other income.

question_text: In the Statement of Profit and Loss, 'Changes in Inventories of Finished Goods, Work-in-Progress and Stock-in-Trade' shows:
explanation: This line item shows the difference between opening and closing inventories. If closing stock > opening stock, it is a negative figure (deducted from expenses) because it represents production not yet sold. If opening stock > closing stock, it is a positive figure (added to expenses).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Difference between opening and closing inventories

elimination_hints.hint: Only closing stock value is shown in the Balance Sheet. The P&L shows the change.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Balance Sheet and P&L treatment.

elimination_hints.hint: Purchases are shown under 'Cost of Materials Consumed', not under changes in inventories.
COGS is not a separate line item in Schedule III format — it is derived from multiple heads.

question_text: Statement: 'Other Income' in the Statement of Profit and Loss includes interest income, dividend income, and net gain on sale of investments.
explanation: This is TRUE. Other Income includes all income that is not derived from the core operations of the business. Examples include interest income on deposits, dividend income from investments, net gain/profit on sale of investments, and rental income (if not the primary business).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: All three items — interest, dividend, and gain on sale of investments — are correctly classified as Other Income for a non-financial company.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking one of these belongs to Revenue from Operations.

question_text: Which of the following expenses is shown SEPARATELY in the Statement of Profit and Loss as per Schedule III?
explanation: As per Schedule III, 'Employee Benefits Expense' is a separate line item in the expense section. It includes salaries, wages, bonus, contribution to provident fund, gratuity, staff welfare expenses, and ESOP compensation. Telephone expense and stationery are part of 'Other Expenses'.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Telephone expenses are minor overhead items shown under 'Other Expenses'.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking all expenses get their own line.

elimination_hints.hint: Printing and stationery falls under 'Other Expenses', not a separate head.
Travelling expenses is part of 'Other Expenses' aggregate.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating every expense as a separate line item.

question_text: The expense heads specifically mentioned in Schedule III of the Statement of Profit and Loss include all EXCEPT:
explanation: Schedule III specifically lists these expense heads: (a) Cost of Materials Consumed, (b) Purchases of Stock-in-Trade, (c) Changes in Inventories, (d) Employee Benefits Expense, (e) Finance Costs, (f) Depreciation and Amortisation, (g) Other Expenses. 'Selling and Distribution Expenses' is not a separate head — it falls under 'Other Expenses'.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Cost of Materials Consumed is the first expense head listed in Schedule III.
Finance Costs is a separately listed expense head covering interest and borrowing costs.
Depreciation and Amortisation is a mandated separate line item.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Finance Costs include interest expense, dividend on redeemable preference shares, and exchange differences to the extent regarded as adjustment to borrowing costs.
Reason (R): Finance Costs represent the cost of borrowed funds used by the company.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation. Finance Costs encompass all costs incurred for borrowings, including interest on loans, dividend on redeemable preference shares (treated as borrowing cost since they must be repaid), and exchange differences arising from foreign currency borrowings that are adjustments to interest cost.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY all those items are classified as Finance Costs — they are all costs of borrowed funds.
R is also correct — Finance Costs do represent the cost of borrowing.
A is factually correct — all listed items are indeed part of Finance Costs per Schedule III.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing redeemable preference dividend as finance cost.

question_text: Profit Before Tax in the Statement of Profit and Loss is calculated as:
explanation: Profit Before Tax = Total Revenue (Revenue from Operations + Other Income) minus Total Expenses. The expenses include cost of materials consumed, purchases of stock-in-trade, changes in inventories, employee benefits, finance costs, depreciation, and other expenses.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Revenue from Operations minus Cost of Goods Sold
Net Sales minus All Expenses including Tax

elimination_hints.hint: COGS is not a separate line item in Schedule III. Also, Other Income must be included in revenue.
Schedule III does not show Gross Profit as a separate line. The format goes directly from revenues to all expenses.
Tax is deducted AFTER Profit Before Tax to arrive at Profit After Tax.

question_text: In the Statement of Profit and Loss, the item 'Purchases of Stock-in-Trade' refers to goods purchased for ______.
explanation: Stock-in-Trade refers to goods purchased for the purpose of resale without any further processing or manufacturing. This is different from raw materials which are purchased for use in manufacturing. Companies that buy and sell goods (trading companies) use this head.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Goods purchased for manufacturing are 'Cost of Materials Consumed', not 'Purchases of Stock-in-Trade'.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing raw materials with trading goods.

elimination_hints.hint: Investments are shown in the Balance Sheet, not as purchases in P&L.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up asset purchases with trading purchases.

elimination_hints.hint: Office supplies fall under 'Other Expenses', not purchases of stock-in-trade.

question_text: A company has Revenue from Operations ₹50,00,000; Other Income ₹2,00,000; Cost of Materials Consumed ₹18,00,000; Employee Benefits ₹8,00,000; Finance Costs ₹3,00,000; Depreciation ₹4,00,000; Other Expenses ₹6,00,000. What is the Profit Before Tax?
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Check your addition — you may have missed including Other Income in Total Revenue.
Verify total expenses — you may have omitted Finance Costs or Depreciation.
Recheck all figures. Total Revenue is ₹52 lakh and Total Expenses is ₹39 lakh.

question_text: Which of the following is shown AFTER 'Profit Before Tax' in the Statement of Profit and Loss?
explanation: After Profit Before Tax (PBT), the next items shown are: (1) Tax Expense comprising Current Tax and Deferred Tax, then (2) Profit/(Loss) for the period from continuing operations, (3) Profit/(Loss) from discontinuing operations, and (4) Earnings Per Share (Basic and Diluted).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Tax Expense (Current Tax and Deferred Tax)

elimination_hints.hint: Depreciation is deducted BEFORE arriving at PBT, not after.
Finance Costs are part of expenses deducted before PBT.
Employee Benefits are operating expenses deducted before PBT.

question_text: Statement: Earnings Per Share (EPS) — both Basic and Diluted — must be disclosed on the face of the Statement of Profit and Loss as per Schedule III.
explanation: This is TRUE. Schedule III mandates that Earnings Per Share (EPS), both Basic and Diluted, be shown on the face of the Statement of Profit and Loss. Basic EPS is calculated on actual shares outstanding, while Diluted EPS considers potential dilutive shares (like convertible debentures, ESOPs).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Schedule III explicitly requires EPS (Basic and Diluted) on the face of P&L, not just in notes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking EPS is optional or only in notes.

question_text: When closing inventory of finished goods exceeds opening inventory, the item 'Changes in Inventories of Finished Goods, WIP and Stock-in-Trade' will be:
explanation: When closing inventory > opening inventory, the change is negative (shown as a deduction from expenses). This is because the increase in inventory means some production was not sold, so the cost should not be charged to P&L. It effectively reduces total expenses. The formula is: Opening Stock – Closing Stock. If closing > opening, the result is negative.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Not shown in the Statement of Profit and Loss

elimination_hints.hint: Positive figure occurs when opening > closing (inventory decreased, meaning more was sold than produced).
It is not shown as income — it remains under expenses but as a negative figure reducing total expenses.
Changes in inventories is a mandatory line item in Schedule III.

question_text:
Match the items with their classification in the Statement of Profit and Loss:

Column I:
(i) Sale of scrap
(ii) Interest on fixed deposits
(iii) Salary and wages
(iv) Interest on debentures

Column II:
(P) Revenue from Operations
(Q) Other Income
(R) Employee Benefits Expense
(S) Finance Costs
explanation: Sale of scrap is part of Revenue from Operations (Other Operating Revenue). Interest on fixed deposits is Other Income (non-operational). Salary and wages fall under Employee Benefits Expense. Interest on debentures is a Finance Cost (cost of borrowed funds).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Sale of scrap is operating revenue (Other Operating Revenue), not Other Income.
Salary and wages are Employee Benefits (R), not Finance Costs (S). Interest on debentures is Finance Costs.
Scrap sale from manufacturing is operational revenue, not other income.
elimination_hints.misconception: Classifying all non-product sales as Other Income.

question_text: Depreciation and Amortisation Expense in the Statement of Profit and Loss includes:
explanation: Depreciation relates to tangible fixed assets (like machinery, building, vehicles) while amortisation relates to intangible assets (like patents, goodwill, trademarks). Together, they represent the systematic allocation of the cost of long-term assets over their useful life.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both depreciation on tangible assets and amortisation of intangible assets

elimination_hints.hint: The line item specifically says 'Depreciation AND Amortisation', indicating both are included.
Same as above — the heading clearly includes both terms.
Impairment losses may be disclosed separately and are not the same as regular depreciation/amortisation.

question_text: A company's Statement of Profit and Loss shows: Revenue from Operations ₹80,00,000; Other Income ₹5,00,000; Total Expenses ₹65,00,000; Current Tax ₹6,00,000; Deferred Tax ₹(50,000). What is the Profit After Tax?
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: You may have ignored the negative Deferred Tax. Net tax = ₹6,00,000 – ₹50,000 = ₹5,50,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Adding deferred tax instead of subtracting.

elimination_hints.hint: Check: did you only deduct Current Tax and forget about Deferred Tax adjustment?
₹20,00,000 is Profit BEFORE Tax. You need to deduct total tax expense.

question_text:
Assertion (A): The Statement of Profit and Loss does not show Gross Profit separately.
Reason (R): Schedule III follows a 'nature of expense' method rather than the 'function of expense' method.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Schedule III adopts the 'nature of expense' method (classifying by nature like materials, employee costs, depreciation) rather than 'function of expense' method (which would show cost of sales, admin expenses, selling expenses, giving Gross Profit). Since expenses are classified by nature, there is no separate Gross Profit line.
subtopic: Nature vs Function of expense method
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY Gross Profit is not shown — because the nature of expense method doesn't separate cost of sales.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding the link between method and format.

elimination_hints.hint: R is factually true — Schedule III does use the nature of expense method.
A is true — there is indeed no separate Gross Profit line in Schedule III format.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking Schedule III shows Gross Profit.

question_text: Which of the following is NOT part of 'Employee Benefits Expense'?
explanation: Directors' sitting fees are paid for attending board meetings and are classified under 'Other Expenses' or disclosed separately as per corporate governance requirements. Employee Benefits Expense includes salaries, wages, bonus, contribution to PF, gratuity expense, staff welfare, and ESOP compensation cost.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: PF contribution is a statutory employee benefit and is part of Employee Benefits Expense.
Staff welfare expenses (medical, canteen, etc.) are part of employee benefits.
Gratuity is a retirement benefit and part of Employee Benefits Expense.

question_text: The Statement of Profit and Loss must show corresponding amounts (figures) for the ______ period alongside the current period figures.
explanation: Schedule III requires comparative figures — the Statement of Profit and Loss must present the corresponding amounts for the immediately preceding reporting period. This helps users compare performance across two periods and assess trends.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Future/next period figures are not available — statements are historical, not predictive.
Budget figures are internal management tools, not required in published financial statements.
Only the immediately preceding period is required for comparison, not the first year.

question_text: In the Statement of Profit and Loss, 'Cost of Materials Consumed' is calculated as:
explanation: Cost of Materials Consumed = Opening Stock of Raw Materials + Purchases of Raw Materials – Closing Stock of Raw Materials. This represents the actual cost of raw materials that were used in the production process during the accounting period.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Opening Stock of RM + Purchases – Closing Stock of RM
Total Purchases made during the year
Opening Stock + Purchases – Closing Stock of Finished Goods

elimination_hints.hint: Total purchases alone doesn't account for opening and closing raw material stock adjustments.
Finished goods stock is dealt with under 'Changes in Inventories', not under Cost of Materials Consumed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing raw materials with finished goods.

elimination_hints.hint: While returns and discounts adjust purchases, the formula must also include opening and closing stock of raw materials.
